items.q: A car turns a corner at constant speed. Is it accelerating?
items.correct: Yes — its velocity changes direction, so it has a centripetal acceleration.
items.misconception: No — constant speed means zero acceleration.
items.random_wrong: Only if the road is banked.
items.why: Students equate acceleration with speeding up, forgetting velocity is a vector that changes when direction changes.

items.q: What 'centrifugal force' flings you outwards on a roundabout?
items.correct: No real outward force acts — you feel your own inertia while a centripetal force pulls you towards the centre.
items.misconception: A real outward centrifugal force acts on you.
items.random_wrong: Gravity reverses direction at high speed.
items.why: The sensation of being thrown outwards leads students to invent an outward force; in an inertial frame only the inward force exists.

items.q: Is momentum lost when two cars crumple together in a crash?
items.correct: No — total momentum is conserved; kinetic energy is transferred to deformation, sound and heat.
items.misconception: Yes — momentum is destroyed by friction and crumpling.
items.random_wrong: No — kinetic energy is conserved instead.
items.why: Students conflate momentum with kinetic energy; KE can be dissipated while the system's momentum is conserved.

items.q: In any collision within an isolated system, which quantity is always conserved?
items.why: Correct answer: Momentum. “Kinetic energy” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: An object moves in a circle at constant speed. Its acceleration is:
items.correct: Directed towards the centre of the circle.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Directed towards the centre of the circle.. “Directed along its velocity.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: The change in momentum. “Force divided by time” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A 2 kg trolley moving at 3 m/s collides with and sticks to a stationary 1 kg trolley. Their combined speed is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 2 m/s. “3 m/s” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is the momentum of a 0.5 kg ball moving at 6 m/s?
items.why: Exact answer: 3 kg m/s. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
